utterances: Another idea behind the camps is ethnic integration.
Relations between minorities and ethnic Georgians are not always smooth.
Marika Bayurmanyan, an Armenian herself, says the camps try to break down those barriers.

Yeah, we have different nationalities and the children, for example, Armenians or others--children are from all parts of Georgia, and they learn how to communicate with each other, with children from different parts of Georgia.
But critics, like opposition lawmaker Paata Zakareishvili, say the Georgian Patriot camps are just an updated version of the Soviet Pioneer camps.
(Through Translator) This is dangerous.
Sakashvili wants to instill these people with his own ideology.
He wants to mobilize the young people so that they don't mobilize against him.

Zakareishvili says the weapons training in the Patriot camps is part of a campaign by the government to encourage militant attitudes and prepare people psychologically for new wars against Georgia's two separatist regions, Abkhazia and South Ossetia.
But even the opposition admits the camps are popular.
Many of the kids are from poor backgrounds.
Drug abuse, unemployment and street crime have exploded in Georgia since the Soviet collapse.
And many parents are happy to keep their kids off the streets, if only for a couple of weeks.
Lawrence Sheets, NPR News, Bakuriani, Georgia.
This is NPR News.
This is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED from NPR News.
I'm Debbie Elliott.
As we've been reporting for the last several months, the fight for the presidency in Mexico has been a real ruckus.
SOUNDBITE OF SKIRMISH
That was the sound of Mexico's Congress yesterday, as President Felipe Calderon took the oath of office.
Supporters of rival candidate Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador were trying to block the inauguration.
To the end, they've insisted that their man really won the July election.
Lopez Obrador even had himself sworn in last month, calling himself the legitimate president.
Mexico's political strife has now migrated to Chicago, albeit in a much quieter form.
Backers of Lopez Obrador's PRD Party have set up an alternate consulate.
NPR's Cheryl Corley has our report.
It was almost a certainty there would be fallout over Mexico's brawling presidential election here in Chicago.
More than a million and a half people of Mexican descent live in the Chicago area.
And now one neighborhood is home to a new diplomatic entity of a sort, an alternate consulate, a small corner storefront building on the city's southwest side.
But it's missing the trademark, which would announce its new status; there's no red, white and green Mexican flag.
Mr.
JORGE MÚJICA (PRD in Illinois): We do have a flag, but it's too fragile for the Chicago winter.
You know, it's a Mexican flag.
SOUNDBITE OF LAUGHTER
Mr. MÚJICA: We don't get this kind of weather so...
Jorge Mújica, a top official of the PRD in Illinois, is a spokesman for the alternate consulate.
Renee Magana is the self-appointed consul.
Although they joke about their protest, both men are serious in their belief that PRD leader Manuel Lopez Obrador was cheated out of the Mexican presidency.
So they followed the steps being taken in Mexico and created their own parallel government entity.
This is not just a rebellion, you know, out of the blue or to have fun.
I mean, this is a serious government plan.
Mujica wants Mexican immigrants to bypass the official consulate altogether.
He says the PRD is working to devise ways to get documents and information directly from state governments in Mexico to streamline an often-clogged process at the consulate.
On Tuesdays, we read from your emails and Web comments.
And last week, it was the New York Post political cartoon that got people talking - two white police officers, one dead chimp, and a reference to the writer of the stimulus bill.

Michael Wohr(ph) emailed from San Francisco to complain: No one who considers world events, history and evolving culture should be unaware of the potentially inflammatory connotations of such an image, especially when monkey images depicting Obama were distributed during the elections.
As an African-American, it never surprises me when people, including other African-Americans, don't recognize the historical underpinnings of images.
Another listener saw the cartoon very differently.
If you want to see a racial slander bad enough, you will.
Just because you have a black president doesn't mean we need to start being oversensitive to edgy humor.
We have much bigger issues to worry about - that from Chris at our Web site.
And news today that Rupert Murdoch apologized for the cartoon.
As head of the News Corporation, he's chairman of the New York Post.
And finally, just before Valentine's Day, we asked you for your best six-word memoirs about love and romance.
From Madeth Phillips(ph) in Wisconsin: My son and daughter have boyfriends.
I cook, you clean, we love - from Michael in Maryland.
And Sophia in Anchorage wrote: Five years and still no proposal.
You can say a lot in six words.
Hundreds of you sent in your own memoirs.
You can read through them at npr.org/talk.
If you have any comments, questions or corrections for us, the best way to reach us is by email.
Our address is talk@npr.org.
Please let us know where you're writing from, and give us some help on how to pronounce your name.

I'm Joe Palca in Washington.

First lady Laura Bush stole the show.

So no one envied comedian and actor Cedric the Entertainer, who had to follow her show-stealing performance.
But being the pro that he is, Ced stepped up to the plate.
Lord.

Condi, how are you?
Con-Condoleezza.

You know, she's very professional, but Condoleezza--come on, right there.
Condoleezza.
It's two people, though.
You know, the name is broken down.
There's the Condi, who's--that's the person you see on television with, you know, the nice hair and very professional.
Then there's the Leezza.

Girl!

In Oregon, a federal judge has opened the door for priest sex abuse victims to sue the Vatican.
NPR's Barbara Bradley Hagerty has the story.
Father Andrew Ronan admitted he abused boys.
When his superiors found out, he was transferred from Ireland to Chicago and finally to Portland, Oregon.
There, says attorney Jeffrey Anderson, he abused again.
He was clearly a serial predator that couldn't control himself.
And in the final analysis the one that could control was the pope.
Anderson contends that Ronan, who died in 1992, was an employee of the Vatican, not just the religious order or diocese he served in, and that the Vatican can be sued.
It's a tough argument, since the Vatican is protected under sovereign immunity.
But this week a federal judge ruled that Anderson has shown evidence there may be a direct tie between the Vatican and Ronan.
And it is thus the Vatican and the pope that is making the decision to keep predator priests in ministry and has the ability to remove them.
That's a leap, says Jeffrey Lena, an attorney for the Vatican.
He says the Vatican knew nothing of Ronan's abuse, and once it found out, it defrocked him.
He says the judge did not rule that Ronan was an employee of the Vatican.
This was a priest of a religious order in the United States who simply had no employment relationship whatsoever with the Holy See.
And if there's no employment relationship there is no case.
Charles Zech at Villanova University agrees.

He says the dioceses and the religious orders are like franchises, and under the judge's ruling, the plaintiffs would have to show that the Vatican was, for example, directing Ronan's transfers around the world.
They'd have to show the Vatican's fingerprints all over this.
Zech says that will be very difficult to do.
And yet, he says, this ruling is a big deal.
For the first time, the Vatican is being required to open its records.
They've resisted that for years, and this is really precedent setting.
The Catholic Church is fighting this vigorously, because if the plaintiffs win this case, countless other alleged victims may themselves try to sue the Vatican directly.
Barbara Bradley Hagerty, NPR News.
Hoping to explore this idea of the solo Thanksgiving, Linton put out a call for stories on Facebook, and got hundreds of responses.
We're going to bring you two of them now.
First, Valerie Baxter in Fairbanks, Alaska, where she is a fur trapper.
Valerie says this is the first time she can remember spending Thanksgiving alone.
And I asked her why this year.
